prompts: A fresh stakeholder request lands mid-project and someone must decide where it ranks against existing work and whether it is worth building at all. On a Scrum Team, which accountability owns that decision?

correct.why: The Product Owner is accountable for maximizing product value and for ordering the Product Backlog, which is where relative priority is decided.

distractors.trap: The Scrum Master enables the process and removes impediments but does not own value or backlog ordering.
Developers decide how to build and how much to pull into a Sprint, not the product value priority.
The sponsor funds and champions the work but sits outside the Scrum Team accountabilities for backlog order.

explanation: Ordering the Product Backlog and maximizing value is the single accountability of the Product Owner.

prompts: Within a Scrum Team, who commits to creating a usable increment each Sprint and decides how the selected work gets built?

correct.why: Developers are the Scrum Team members who commit to producing the increment and own how the work is done.

distractors.trap: The Product Owner decides what and in what order, not how the increment is engineered.
The Scrum Master supports the process and removes impediments but does not build the increment.
A functional manager sits outside the Scrum Team accountabilities for the increment.

explanation: Developers are accountable for creating the increment and for the how of the work selected into the Sprint.

eco_task: Remove impediments and blockers for the team
prompts: A Developer reports that a missing test environment is blocking progress and the team cannot resolve it alone. Whose accountability is it to help clear this obstacle?

correct.why: Removing impediments that the team cannot resolve on its own is a core Scrum Master accountability.

distractors.trap: The Product Owner orders value; clearing process and environment blockers is not their accountability.
The sponsor may fund a fix but is not the role tasked with day-to-day impediment removal.
A QA lead checks quality, not the role accountable for removing team impediments.

explanation: Impediment removal that supports team flow is a defining responsibility of the Scrum Master.

prompts: A team feels that handoffs between testers and Developers are slow and wants a regular forum to surface and fix how they collaborate. Which event is designed for this purpose?

correct.why: The retrospective is the recurring event for the team to inspect and improve its own way of working.

distractors.trap: The review inspects the product increment with stakeholders, not the internal collaboration.
The Daily Scrum synchronizes near-term work; it is not the forum for broader process improvement.
Planning selects upcoming work and is not aimed at reflecting on team process.

explanation: Improving how the team works is the purpose of the retrospective.

prompts: A leader spends most of their time clearing obstacles, coaching, and giving the team authority to decide, rather than issuing detailed instructions. Which leadership style is this?

correct.why: Servant leadership focuses on enabling, supporting, and developing the team rather than directing it.

distractors.trap: Command and control relies on top-down direction and close supervision, the opposite of the described behavior.
The leader is actively coaching and removing impediments, not disengaged.
This style intervenes mainly on deviations; it is not the enabling, team-developing approach described.

explanation: Enabling and supporting the team to own its work is the hallmark of servant leadership.

prompts: A team values a member who is deeply expert in testing yet can also pick up some development and analysis work when the team needs it. Which term best describes this person?

correct.why: A generalizing specialist has deep expertise in one area plus enough breadth to contribute across others.

distractors.trap: A pure specialist is deep in one domain only, without the breadth described.
A functional manager leads a discipline; the description is about an individual contributor with depth and breadth.
The Product Owner role is about product value and ordering, not about having cross-skill depth and breadth.

explanation: Deep in one area with useful breadth across others describes a generalizing specialist, often called T-shaped.

prompts: Sitting together, team members pick up useful context simply by overhearing nearby conversations, without any meeting being called. Which communication concept is this?

correct.why: Osmotic communication is the informal absorption of useful information from being near others and overhearing relevant exchanges.

distractors.trap: Formal communication is deliberately structured through planned channels and documents, not ambient overhearing.
An information radiator is a visible display; osmotic communication is absorbed by proximity and overhearing.
Status reporting is a periodic deliberate communication, not passive absorption from proximity.

explanation: Absorbing information passively through proximity and overhearing is osmotic communication.

prompts: Select the three accountabilities that together make up a Scrum Team.

correct.why: The Product Owner is one of the three Scrum Team accountabilities, owning product value and backlog order.
The Scrum Master is one of the three Scrum Team accountabilities, enabling the framework and removing impediments.
The Developers are one of the three Scrum Team accountabilities, committing to create the increment.

distractors.trap: The project manager is not a Scrum Team accountability within the Scrum framework.
The sponsor is a stakeholder outside the Scrum Team, not one of its accountabilities.
Business analyst is not a defined Scrum Team accountability, though such skills may sit within Developers.

explanation: A Scrum Team is the Product Owner, the Scrum Master, and the Developers; other titles sit outside these accountabilities.

prompts: A new system is fully deployed, but staff keep slipping back to the old process and say they miss the way things used to work. They have not let go of the old way. Which change model best explains what they are experiencing?

correct.why: Bridges separates the external change from the internal psychological transition; clinging to the old way and grieving it is the Ending phase that must be worked through first.

distractors.trap: Lewin describes the external process of unfreeze-change-refreeze, not the emotional act of letting go of the old identity.
ADKAR would label a missing Desire or Knowledge outcome; it does not frame the experience as an emotional Ending and Neutral Zone.
Kotter prescribes organization-wide leadership steps from the top; it is not a description of an individual mourning the old state.

explanation: Bridges is the model that distinguishes the external change event from the internal transition people pass through: Ending, Neutral Zone, New Beginning. Resistance from not having let go is the Ending phase.

prompts: Which change model targets the personal adoption journey of a single individual through five sequential outcomes rather than steps an organization takes?

correct.why: ADKAR is individual-level: Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement are the five outcomes each person must reach to adopt a change.

distractors.trap: Kotter operates at the organization level through leadership action, not the personal transition of one individual.
Lewin describes the change process in three broad phases for the system, not five outcomes inside a person.
Satir tracks a group's performance dip and recovery curve, not an individual's sequence of adoption outcomes.

explanation: ADKAR is the diagnostic that works at the individual level. Kotter and Lewin are organization or process focused; Satir describes a system performance curve.

prompts: Which change model is defined by exactly eight prescriptive steps, beginning with creating a sense of urgency and ending with anchoring the change in the culture?

correct.why: Kotter is the eight-step, leadership-driven sequence for organization-wide change that runs from creating urgency to anchoring it in the culture.

distractors.trap: Lewin has three stages, not eight: unfreeze, change, refreeze.
ADKAR has five outcomes, not eight steps, and works at the individual level.
Bridges has three phases of psychological transition, not eight organizational steps.

explanation: Counting is the quickest tell: Kotter = 8 steps, ADKAR = 5 elements, Lewin and Bridges = 3 each.

prompts: After a disruptive change, a team's performance drops into a period of chaos and confusion before recovering to a new, higher level of capability. Which model describes this dip-and-recovery curve?

correct.why: Satir is the five-stage model focused on how a group's performance dips into chaos and then recovers as it absorbs a disruptive change.

distractors.trap: Bridges describes an individual's psychological phases of letting go, not a measurable system performance curve.
Lewin frames the change as unfreeze-change-refreeze; it does not center on the performance dip and recovery.
Kotter is a leadership action plan, not a description of a performance trough during change.

explanation: The performance dip into chaos before recovery is the signature of the Satir model. Bridges, its closest confusable, deals with individual emotional phases instead.

prompts: Lewin's model unfreezes the status quo and makes the change. What is the third stage that locks in the new state so people do not slide back?

correct.why: Refreeze is Lewin's third stage; it stabilizes and embeds the new state so the change holds.

distractors.trap: Reinforcement is the final element of ADKAR, not the third stage of Lewin's model.

distractors.text: Anchor the change in the culture
distractors.trap: Anchoring in the culture is Kotter's eighth step, not Lewin's third stage.
New Beginning is the final phase of the Bridges transition model, not a Lewin stage.

explanation: Lewin is unfreeze, change, refreeze. The distractors are the analogous anti-reversion ideas from other models, which is exactly the trap.

prompts: Lewin's Refreeze keeps a change from sliding back to the old state. Which ADKAR element plays this same anti-reversion role at the individual level?

correct.why: Reinforcement is the final ADKAR outcome; it sustains the change and prevents the individual from reverting, mirroring Lewin's Refreeze.

distractors.trap: Ability is about being able to perform the change day to day, not about sustaining it against backsliding.
Awareness is the first ADKAR outcome about understanding the need for change, the opposite end from sustaining it.
Desire is the personal motivation to support the change, not the mechanism that locks it in afterward.

explanation: Refreeze (Lewin) and Reinforcement (ADKAR) are the mirror anti-reversion concepts at the system versus individual level.

prompts: In the Bridges Transition Model, how do change and transition differ?

correct.text: Change is the external event that happens; transition is the internal psychological process people go through to come to terms with it
correct.why: Bridges deliberately separates the external situational change from the slower internal transition (Ending, Neutral Zone, New Beginning) people must work through.

distractors.text: Change is the internal mindset shift; transition is the external rollout of new tools and processes
distractors.trap: This reverses the Bridges definitions; the internal piece is the transition, the external piece is the change.

distractors.text: Change and transition are synonyms used interchangeably in the model
distractors.trap: The whole point of Bridges is that they are not the same thing; conflating them misses the model's core idea.

distractors.text: Change is gradual and planned; transition is sudden and unplanned
distractors.trap: Neither term is defined by speed or planning in Bridges; the distinction is external event versus internal experience.

explanation: Bridges: change is external and can happen quickly, but transition is the internal, emotional adjustment that takes people longer.

prompts: A project manager is building communication, training, and stakeholder adoption plans to help users embrace a new system and reach the future state. Which discipline is this?

correct.why: Change management is the structured, people-focused approach for transitioning individuals and groups from a current state to a future state with intended benefits.

distractors.trap: Change control assesses and approves proposed changes to project work and baselines; it is not about people adopting the outcome.
Integrated change control coordinates evaluating and approving change requests across the project, not training and adoption of the delivered solution.
Configuration management controls the versions and attributes of products and documents, not human adoption of a new system.

explanation: Change management = people, adoption, transition. Change control and its relatives = governing modifications to baselines and deliverables.

prompts: A stakeholder submits a formal request to modify the scope baseline, and the project manager routes it for impact assessment and approval before any work changes. Which discipline governs this?

correct.why: Change control assesses and authorizes proposed changes to project work and baselines before they are implemented.

distractors.trap: Change management is about people adopting and sustaining the future state, not the formal evaluation of a baseline change request.
Organizational change concerns shifts in how the organization operates and behaves, not the approval of a project baseline modification.
Benefits realization tracks whether the intended value is achieved, not the assessment and approval of a scope change.

explanation: The mirror of the previous question: routing a baseline change request for assessment and approval is change control, not the people-side change management.

eco_task: Plan and manage transition to operations
prompts: Testing confirms the new platform meets every agreed requirement, yet the support desk is untrained, runbooks are missing, and users have had no preparation. Which condition has NOT yet been met?

correct.why: Operational readiness is the state in which the organization, users, and support model are prepared to receive, operate, and sustain the deliverable - which is exactly what is missing here.

distractors.trap: Acceptance confirms the deliverable meets agreed criteria, and the scenario says testing already passed - so acceptance is satisfied.
Verification checks the product was built right against requirements; that has been done. The gap is organizational preparedness.
Definition of done confirms an increment is complete; the build is complete here. The missing piece is the organization being ready to use it.

explanation: A deliverable can be accepted and verified yet still not be ready for live use. Operational readiness is about the receiving organization, not the product itself.

eco_task: Plan and manage transition to operations
prompts: An assessment is run as the team approaches deployment to gauge how prepared the organization is to integrate and sustain the new solution. What is this activity?

correct.why: A readiness assessment evaluates organizational preparedness to integrate and sustain the solution as deployment approaches.

distractors.trap: Acceptance testing evaluates whether the solution itself meets requirements, not whether the organization is prepared to absorb it.
Gap analysis compares current and future states to size the work; it is not the deployment-time check of organizational preparedness.
Commissioning verifies that installed systems function as designed, not that the people and organization are ready to operate them.

explanation: Readiness assessment looks at the organization; acceptance testing looks at the product. The two answer different questions at deployment.

eco_task: Plan and manage transition to operations
prompts: For the first two weeks immediately after go-live, an elevated support team stands by to resolve issues quickly and stabilize operations before normal support takes over. What is this period called?

correct.why: Hypercare is the short, intensified support period right after go-live to resolve issues fast and stabilize operations.

distractors.trap: Operations support is the ongoing steady-state service model, not the short high-attention stabilization window after deployment.
A warranty period is a contractual defect-remedy window, not the staffed intensive stabilization effort hypercare describes.
Cutover is the switchover moment to the new system, not the support-intensive period that follows go-live.

explanation: Hypercare is the temporary, high-touch bridge from go-live to steady-state operations support.

eco_task: Plan and manage transition to operations
prompts: Within the broader go-live event, which term names the specific switchover act where the new system is turned on and the old one is retired?

correct.why: Cutover is the specific transition point at which the new system goes live and replaces the old one.

distractors.trap: Go-live is the broader event of becoming operational; cutover is the precise switchover act that achieves it.
Hypercare is the intensive support that follows the switchover, not the switchover itself.
Release is the decision or event that makes a deliverable available, not the operational switch from old to new system.

explanation: Go-live is the umbrella moment of becoming operational; cutover is the narrow technical switchover inside it.

eco_task: Plan and manage transition to operations
prompts: A deliverable is formally accepted and ownership shifts from the project team to the operations group, who will now run and sustain it. The single act of shifting accountability is best described as which term?

correct.why: Transfer of responsibility is the specific point at which ownership, custody, or accountability for a deliverable shifts from one party to another.

distractors.trap: Transition is the broader process of moving outputs into operational use and adoption; the narrow shift of accountability is the transfer of responsibility within it.
Benefits realization is about achieving intended value over time, not the moment accountability changes hands.
Project closure is the administrative completion of the project or phase, not specifically the accountability handoff of a deliverable.

explanation: Transfer of responsibility is the precise accountability shift; transition is the broader move into operational use that contains it.

eco_task: Plan and manage transition to operations
prompts: Which document specifically describes how project outputs and capabilities will be handed into operational use and sustained after the project ends?

correct.why: The transition plan focuses specifically on how outputs are transferred into operational use and sustained after project or phase completion.

distractors.trap: The project management plan governs how the project is managed overall; it does not focus narrowly on operational handoff and adoption.
A release plan is about delivering increments into use; the transition plan covers the broader handoff to and sustainment by operations.
A benefits realization plan tracks how and when value is achieved, not the mechanics of handing the solution to operations.

explanation: The transition plan is the dedicated artifact for operational handoff and sustainment, distinct from the overarching project management plan.

eco_task: Plan and manage transition to operations
prompts: A solution is fully built and passes every test, yet leadership doubts the workforce can absorb and sustain it. Which kind of readiness is in question?

correct.why: Organisational readiness is the degree to which the organization is prepared to adopt, absorb, and sustain a change - which is precisely the doubt raised.

distractors.trap: Solution readiness asks whether the deliverable itself is complete; the scenario says it is built and tested, so that is not the gap.
Definition of Ready is an agile entry checklist for whether a backlog item is ready to be worked, unrelated to organizational absorption of a change.
Schedule readiness is not the concern; the issue is whether people and the organization can absorb and sustain the change.

explanation: Solution readiness is about the product; organisational readiness is about the receiving organization's capacity to adopt and sustain it.

eco_task: Plan and manage transition to operations
prompts: On a facility project, the structure is fully built, but before handover to operations a structured process verifies and documents that the installed systems actually function as designed. What is this process?

correct.why: Commissioning is the structured process of verifying and documenting that installed systems function as designed before handover to operations.

distractors.trap: Construction completion means the asset is built; commissioning goes further to prove the built asset actually works as intended.
Cutover is the switchover to live operation, not the verification that installed systems perform to design.
Acceptance is the formal confirmation that criteria are met; commissioning is the functional proving activity that often feeds it.

explanation: Built is not the same as proven to work. Commissioning is the functional verification step between construction completion and operational handover.

eco_task: Manage product or asset end of life
prompts: A legacy product reaches the end of its useful life and the organization decides to withdraw it from operational use. The broad end-of-life decision and state is best termed which of the following?

correct.why: Retirement is the broad planned withdrawal of a product, service, or capability from operational use at the end of its useful life.

distractors.trap: Decommissioning is one action used to execute the retirement; it is the narrower technical step, not the broad end-of-life decision.
Cutover is the switchover to a new system going live, not the withdrawal of an old product at end of life.
Closure finalizes a project or phase administratively; it is not the end-of-life withdrawal of an operational product.

explanation: Retirement is the broad end-of-life state and decision; decommissioning is a concrete action that carries it out.

prompts: Three of the following are stages of one change model. Which item belongs to a DIFFERENT model and is the odd one out?

correct.why: Neutral Zone is a phase of the Bridges Transition Model; the other three (Unfreeze, Change, Refreeze) all belong to Lewin.

distractors.trap: Unfreeze is Lewin's first stage; it is part of the group of three, not the odd one out.
Change is Lewin's middle stage; it belongs with Unfreeze and Refreeze.
Refreeze is Lewin's third stage; it belongs with the other two Lewin stages.

explanation: Unfreeze-Change-Refreeze are Lewin's three stages. Neutral Zone is the middle phase of Bridges, making it the outsider.

prompts: Select the three phases that make up the Bridges Transition Model.

correct.why: The first Bridges phase is about endings - letting go of the old way before anything new can begin.
The Neutral Zone is the in-between phase of confusion and recalibration after the ending and before the new beginning.
The New Beginning is the final phase where people accept the new identity and direction.

distractors.trap: Unfreeze is the opening stage of Lewin's model, not a Bridges phase.
Refreeze is Lewin's closing stage, not part of the Bridges transition phases.
Reinforcement is the last element of ADKAR, not a Bridges phase.

explanation: Bridges = Ending/Letting Go, Neutral Zone, New Beginning. The traps pull in Lewin and ADKAR terms that sound like sustainment.

prompts: Which TWO change models operate primarily at the level of the individual rather than the whole organization?

correct.why: ADKAR diagnoses and supports change within each individual through five personal outcomes.
Bridges focuses on the internal psychological transition each person passes through, an individual-level lens.

distractors.trap: Kotter is organization-level, led from the top through eight leadership steps.
Lewin describes the change process for the system or organization, not the inner experience of one person.
Satir tracks a group or system's performance through a disruptive change, not an individual's outcomes.

explanation: ADKAR (adoption outcomes) and Bridges (psychological transition) are individual-focused; Kotter, Lewin, and Satir look at the organization or system.

prompts: A sponsor keeps explaining why the change matters and what will happen to the business if nothing is done, aiming to build each person's personal motivation to support it. Which ADKAR element is being addressed?

correct.why: Desire is the personal willingness and motivation to support and participate in the change, which is what the sponsor is cultivating.

distractors.trap: Awareness is just knowing the change and its need exist; building the motivation to act on it is the next outcome, Desire.
Knowledge is knowing how to change; the sponsor here is building the want-to, not the how-to.
Reinforcement sustains the change after adoption; the sponsor is still working to create initial motivation.

explanation: Building personal motivation and the will to change is the Desire element of ADKAR, which follows Awareness.

prompts: What is the first step of Kotter's 8-Step Model for leading organizational change?

correct.why: Kotter's model begins by establishing a sense of urgency so the organization is motivated to start the change.

distractors.trap: Building the guiding coalition is Kotter's second step, which comes after urgency is established.

distractors.text: Anchor the changes in the culture
distractors.trap: Anchoring in the culture is Kotter's final, eighth step, not the first.
Unfreeze is the first stage of Lewin's model, not Kotter's first step.

explanation: Kotter starts with creating urgency. The trap mixes in a later Kotter step and Lewin's opening stage.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes Maslow hierarchy of needs from Herzberg two-factor theory?

correct.text: Maslow ranks all needs on one ascending ladder, while Herzberg splits factors into hygiene factors and motivators
correct.why: Maslow uses a single ascending hierarchy; Herzberg places factors into two independent categories with different effects on motivation.

distractors.text: Maslow splits factors into hygiene and motivators, while Herzberg uses an ascending ladder
distractors.trap: Swaps the two theories around, this describes them in reverse.

distractors.text: Both describe three acquired needs that vary by individual
distractors.trap: That is McClelland, neither Maslow nor Herzberg uses three acquired needs.

distractors.text: Both focus on the perceived link between effort, performance, and reward
distractors.trap: That is expectancy theory, not Maslow or Herzberg.

explanation: Maslow is one ladder; Herzberg is two separate buckets. Confusing the structure of the two is the most common trap.

prompts: A manager assumes team members are inherently lazy, avoid responsibility, and must be closely supervised. Which theory describes this assumption?

correct.why: Theory X is the management assumption that workers are lazy, avoid responsibility, and need close control and supervision.

distractors.trap: Theory Y is the opposite assumption, that workers are self-motivated and seek responsibility.
Hygiene factors concern dissatisfaction, not assumptions about whether workers are lazy.
Need for power is an individual drive, not a managerial assumption about the workforce.

explanation: Theory X equals distrust and control; Theory Y equals trust and self-direction. The lazy-and-needs-supervision framing is pure Theory X.

prompts: A project manager trusts the team to direct itself, delegates broadly, and assumes members willingly accept responsibility. This leadership stance is grounded in which assumption set?

correct.why: Theory Y assumes workers are self-motivated, accept responsibility, and can be trusted to direct themselves, which fits a delegating, trust-based stance.

distractors.trap: Theory X assumes workers must be controlled and coerced, the opposite of this trust-based stance.
Safety needs are one level of Maslow ladder, not a managerial assumption set about self-direction.
Coercive power compels through penalties, contradicting the trusting, delegating behaviour described.

explanation: Trust, delegation, and belief in self-direction are the hallmarks of Theory Y, which pairs naturally with servant and self-organizing-team approaches.

prompts: A team member is energized mainly by setting and beating challenging targets rather than by relationships or by influencing others. In McClelland terms, which need is dominant?

correct.why: McClelland need for achievement is the drive to set and reach challenging goals, distinct from affiliation and power.

distractors.trap: Affiliation is the drive for relationships and belonging, not for beating targets.
Need for power is the drive to influence or control others, not to achieve personal targets.
Esteem is a Maslow level, not one of McClelland three acquired needs.

explanation: McClelland names three acquired needs, achievement, affiliation, and power. Goal-chasing for its own sake is the achievement profile.

prompts: A team member will exert effort only when they believe the effort will produce good performance and that performance will lead to a reward they value. Which theory captures this chain?

correct.why: Expectancy theory holds that motivation depends on expectancy that effort yields performance, instrumentality that performance yields reward, and valence of that reward.

distractors.trap: Herzberg separates hygiene factors from motivators and does not model the effort-performance-reward chain.
Maslow ranks intrinsic needs and does not turn on perceived effort-to-reward links.
Theory Y is an assumption about worker self-direction, not a model of expectancy and valence.

explanation: Expectancy theory is the calculation model, motivation equals expectancy times instrumentality times valence. The effort-performance-reward language is the giveaway.

prompts: Three of the following are levels in Maslow hierarchy of needs. Which one is NOT a Maslow level?

correct.why: Instrumentality is a component of Vroom expectancy theory, not a level in Maslow hierarchy.

distractors.trap: Safety is the second level of Maslow hierarchy.
Esteem is the fourth level of Maslow hierarchy.
Self-actualization is the top level of Maslow hierarchy.

explanation: Maslow levels are physiological, safety, social or belonging, esteem, and self-actualization. Instrumentality belongs to expectancy theory.

prompts: For complex, creative work, a project leader emphasizes giving the team autonomy, the chance to build mastery, and a sense of purpose, rather than cash bonuses. Which model is being applied?

correct.why: Pink argues that for complex work, the intrinsic drivers of autonomy, mastery, and purpose outperform external rewards.

distractors.trap: Herzberg distinguishes hygiene from motivators generally but does not name autonomy, mastery, and purpose.
McClelland names achievement, affiliation, and power, not autonomy, mastery, and purpose.
Expectancy theory is about effort-reward links, not the three intrinsic drivers Pink describes.

explanation: Autonomy, mastery, and purpose is the Pink Drive triad for intrinsic motivation on creative work.

prompts: Which pairing of theory to its central idea is correct?

correct.text: McGregor pairs with Theory X and Theory Y assumptions about workers
correct.why: McGregor authored Theory X and Theory Y, the contrasting assumptions about whether workers are lazy or self-directed.

distractors.text: Maslow pairs with hygiene factors and motivators
distractors.trap: Hygiene and motivators belong to Herzberg, not Maslow.

distractors.text: Herzberg pairs with autonomy, mastery, and purpose
distractors.trap: Autonomy, mastery, and purpose is Pink Drive, not Herzberg.

distractors.text: Vroom pairs with achievement, affiliation, and power
distractors.trap: Achievement, affiliation, and power is McClelland, not Vroom.

explanation: Who-said-what check, McGregor owns Theory X and Y. The distractors mismatch each theorist with a rival theory.

prompts: How does McClelland theory of needs differ from Maslow hierarchy of needs?

correct.text: McClelland focuses on three learned needs whose balance varies by individual, while Maslow arranges universal needs in an ascending hierarchy
correct.why: McClelland needs are acquired and vary in proportion per person; Maslow needs are universal and ordered as a ladder.

distractors.text: McClelland arranges needs in an ascending ladder, while Maslow describes three learned needs
distractors.trap: Reverses the two, the ladder is Maslow and the three learned needs are McClelland.

distractors.text: Both split factors into hygiene and motivators
distractors.trap: Neither does, that two-category split is Herzberg.

distractors.text: Both model the link between effort, performance, and reward
distractors.trap: That is expectancy theory, not McClelland or Maslow.

explanation: Maslow is a universal ladder; McClelland is a personal mix of three acquired needs.

prompts: A project manager with no formal authority over a vendor team gains their cooperation because they respect the manager deep technical knowledge. Which power base is in play?

correct.why: Expert power is influence derived from specialized knowledge or skill, independent of formal title.

distractors.trap: Legitimate power comes from formal position, which the manager does not hold over the vendor.
Reward power works through granting benefits, not through respected expertise.
Referent power comes from charisma and admiration of character, not from demonstrated knowledge.

explanation: Influence rooted in what you know is expert power; influence rooted in who you are is referent power. The cue here is technical knowledge.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes legitimate power from expert power?

correct.text: Legitimate power comes from formal position, while expert power comes from demonstrated knowledge regardless of title
correct.why: Legitimate power is granted by role; expert power is earned through competence and exists independent of position.

distractors.text: Legitimate power comes from knowledge, while expert power comes from formal position
distractors.trap: Reverses the two definitions.

distractors.text: Both are earned through personal charisma
distractors.trap: Charisma is referent power, and legitimate power is granted, not earned.

distractors.text: Both are granted by the organization through the role
distractors.trap: Expert power is earned by competence, not granted by the role.

explanation: Position versus knowledge is the dividing line. Legitimate is granted by role; expert is earned by competence.

prompts: Of the five power bases, which two are personal and earned by the individual rather than granted by the organizational position? Select two.

correct.why: Expert power is earned through specialized knowledge and competence, not conferred by a title.
Referent power is earned through character, charisma, and the respect others hold, not by formal role.

distractors.trap: Legitimate power is granted by the formal position, not earned personally.
Reward power stems from the organizational ability to grant benefits, a positional lever rather than a personal one.
Coercive power stems from the positional ability to impose penalties, not from personal qualities.

explanation: Expert and referent power are earned and personal; legitimate, reward, and coercive flow from position. Expert and referent are also generally the most effective.

prompts: Which of the five power bases is generally considered the least effective and most likely to damage team relationships when overused?

correct.why: Coercive power relies on the threat of penalties and tends to be the least effective, breeding resentment and damaging trust when relied on.

distractors.trap: Expert power, based on respected knowledge, is among the most effective, not the least.
Referent power, based on admiration and trust, is among the most effective.
Reward power uses positive incentives, so it is generally more effective than coercion.

explanation: Coercion, ruling by penalty, is the weakest lever long term; expert and referent power are the strongest.

prompts: A team complies with a leader chiefly because they admire her character and want to be associated with her. Which power base is this?

correct.why: Referent power is influence derived from charisma, character, and the respect and admiration others hold for the person.

distractors.trap: Expert power is based on specialized knowledge, not admiration of character.
Legitimate power flows from formal position, not personal admiration.
Reward power works through granting incentives, not through being admired.

explanation: Admiration and wanting to be associated with the person is referent power; knowledge would be expert power.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes reward power from coercive power?

correct.text: Reward power motivates with positive incentives, while coercive power compels through the threat of penalties
correct.why: Reward and coercive power are mirror images, one offers benefits and the other threatens consequences.

distractors.text: Reward power threatens penalties, while coercive power offers incentives
distractors.trap: Reverses the two, reward offers incentives and coercive threatens penalties.

distractors.text: Reward power is earned by expertise, while coercive power is earned by charisma
distractors.trap: Both are positional powers, not earned by expertise or charisma.

distractors.text: Both come from the leader specialized knowledge
distractors.trap: That is expert power, not reward or coercive.

explanation: Carrot versus stick, reward grants benefits and coercion threatens penalties; both derive from position, not personal qualities.

prompts: How does power differ from authority in a project environment?

correct.text: Power is the broader capacity to influence outcomes, while authority is formally granted decision rights within a role
correct.why: Authority is the formal slice of decision rights given by the role; power is the wider ability to influence resources, priorities, and behaviour.

distractors.text: Power is formally granted decision rights, while authority is the broader capacity to influence
distractors.trap: Reverses the relationship, authority is the granted formal piece, power is the broader capacity.

distractors.text: Power and authority are identical and interchangeable
distractors.trap: They overlap but are not the same, one can have power without formal authority.

distractors.text: Authority is earned through expertise, while power is granted by position
distractors.trap: Confuses the concepts, authority is the granted formal right here, not the earned one.

explanation: Authority is a formal subset of decision rights; power is the larger ability to influence, which may exist with or without authority.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes servant leadership from Situational Leadership II?

correct.text: Servant leadership is a consistent values-based orientation toward serving the team, while Situational Leadership II prescribes switching styles by follower readiness
correct.why: Servant leadership keeps a steady serve-the-team stance; Situational Leadership II deliberately changes style based on a follower competence and commitment on each task.

distractors.text: Servant leadership switches styles by follower readiness, while Situational Leadership II is a fixed values-based orientation
distractors.trap: Reverses the two, style switching by readiness is Situational Leadership II.

distractors.text: Both are hands-off styles that leave the team fully unsupervised
distractors.trap: That describes laissez-faire, not these two models.

distractors.text: Both prescribe top-down command and close supervision
distractors.trap: That is command and control, the opposite of servant leadership.

explanation: Servant leadership is a steady orientation; Situational Leadership II is adaptive style matching to follower readiness on a task.

prompts: A capable, self-managing team is given wide autonomy, and the leader deliberately steps back and intervenes very little. Which leadership style is this?

correct.why: Laissez-faire is a hands-off style in which the leader grants wide autonomy and intervenes little, which can suit a mature, capable team.

distractors.trap: Directing closely instructs and controls the work, the opposite of stepping back.
Coercive leadership compels through penalties, not by granting autonomy.
Transactional leadership exchanges rewards for performance, not a hands-off grant of autonomy.

explanation: Hands-off and steps back is laissez-faire; closely instructs is directing. Match style to team maturity.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes leadership from management?

correct.text: Leadership inspires and aligns people toward a shared vision, while management organizes and controls work to meet defined objectives
correct.why: Leadership focuses on direction, influence, and alignment; management focuses on organizing, controlling, and administering the work.

distractors.text: Leadership organizes and controls work, while management inspires toward a shared vision
distractors.trap: Reverses the two, inspiring toward a vision is leadership.

distractors.text: Leadership and management are identical activities
distractors.trap: They are complementary but distinct, with different emphases.

distractors.text: Leadership applies only to agile teams, while management applies only to predictive teams
distractors.trap: Both leadership and management apply across delivery approaches.

explanation: Leaders inspire and align; managers organize and control. The PMP expects you to balance both.

prompts: A coach guides a team member through clarifying the Goal, examining current Reality, exploring Options, and confirming the Will to act. Which coaching model is being used?

correct.why: GROW is a four-stage coaching framework structured around Goal, Reality, Options, and Will.

distractors.trap: OSCAR uses Outcome, Situation, Choices, Actions, and Review, not Goal-Reality-Options-Will.
ADKAR is an individual change model, Awareness Desire Knowledge Ability Reinforcement, not a coaching conversation framework.
Tuckman describes team development stages, not a coaching conversation structure.

explanation: Goal, Reality, Options, Will spells GROW. OSCAR is the rival coaching model that adds a Review stage.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes the GROW coaching model from the OSCAR coaching model?

correct.text: GROW ends at securing the will to act, while OSCAR explicitly builds in a Review stage for accountability
correct.why: Both are coaching frameworks, but OSCAR adds an explicit Review step for follow-up and reinforcement that GROW does not name separately.

distractors.text: OSCAR ends at securing the will to act, while GROW adds an explicit Review stage
distractors.trap: Reverses the models, the explicit Review stage belongs to OSCAR.

distractors.text: GROW is a change model and OSCAR is a power base
distractors.trap: Both are coaching frameworks, neither is a change model or a power base.

distractors.text: Both are leadership styles matched to follower maturity
distractors.trap: That describes situational leadership, not these coaching models.

explanation: GROW stops at Will; OSCAR carries through to an explicit Review for accountability and reinforcement.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes mentoring from coaching?

correct.text: Mentoring is broad and experience-driven over time, while coaching is targeted at improving specific performance or skills
correct.why: Mentoring shares an experienced person guidance broadly over time; coaching draws out solutions to improve specific, near-term performance.

distractors.text: Mentoring is targeted at a specific skill, while coaching is a broad lifelong relationship
distractors.trap: Reverses the two, coaching is the targeted, performance-focused approach.

distractors.text: Mentoring imposes penalties, while coaching grants rewards
distractors.trap: Confuses developmental approaches with power bases.
They differ in breadth and time horizon, mentoring is broader and longer.

explanation: Mentoring is broad and experience-led; coaching is focused and draws solutions out of the person. Easy to confuse.

prompts: A newly formed team is openly arguing over roles, priorities, and approach. Which Tuckman stage is the team in?

correct.why: Storming is the Tuckman stage marked by conflict over roles, priorities, and approach as the team works out how to operate.

distractors.trap: Forming is the initial polite, orientation stage before conflict surfaces.
Norming is when the team settles into agreed ways of working, after the conflict of storming.
Performing is the high-output mature stage, not the conflict stage.

explanation: Tuckman runs forming, storming, norming, performing, adjourning. Open conflict over roles is storming.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes Tuckman stages of team development from the Drexler-Sibbet team performance model?

correct.text: Tuckman describes five descriptive stages of group maturity, while Drexler-Sibbet maps seven stages each framed by a question the team must resolve
correct.why: Tuckman is five descriptive maturity stages; Drexler-Sibbet uses seven stages, each anchored by a specific question the team resolves.

distractors.text: Tuckman maps seven question-based stages, while Drexler-Sibbet lists five descriptive stages
distractors.trap: Reverses the two, the seven question-based stages belong to Drexler-Sibbet.

distractors.text: Both are coaching conversation frameworks like GROW
distractors.trap: Both are team development models, not coaching conversation frameworks.

distractors.text: Both prescribe leadership styles by follower maturity
distractors.trap: That is situational leadership, not these team development models.

explanation: Five descriptive stages is Tuckman; seven question-anchored stages is Drexler-Sibbet.

prompts: The PMI Talent Triangle identifies three skill domains that project professionals need. Select the three domains.

correct.why: Ways of working is one of the three PMI Talent Triangle domains, covering delivery approaches and technical practice.
Power skills, the interpersonal abilities, are one of the three Talent Triangle domains.
Business acumen, strategic and organizational understanding, is the third Talent Triangle domain.

distractors.trap: Coercive influence is a power base, not a Talent Triangle domain.
Emotional intelligence sits within power skills but is not itself one of the three named domains.

explanation: The PMI Talent Triangle is ways of working, power skills, and business acumen. Power skills is one side, not the whole triangle.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes power skills from business acumen?

correct.text: Power skills are interpersonal abilities for working with people, while business acumen is strategic understanding of the organization
correct.why: Power skills cover communication and leadership with people; business acumen is the understanding of the organization and its environment to drive value.

distractors.text: Power skills are strategic understanding of the organization, while business acumen is interpersonal ability
distractors.trap: Reverses the two, the interpersonal side is power skills.

distractors.text: Both are the same side of the PMI Talent Triangle
distractors.trap: They are two distinct sides of the Talent Triangle, not the same one.

distractors.text: Both refer to technical delivery methods and tools
distractors.trap: Technical methods are ways of working, not power skills or business acumen.

explanation: Power skills equal people skills; business acumen equals organizational and strategic understanding. They are different sides of the triangle.

prompts: A learner has just become aware of how much they do not know about a new tool and feels clumsy using it. In the four stages of competence, which stage is this?

correct.why: Conscious incompetence is when a learner becomes aware of their skill gap, which often feels clumsy or discouraging before practice builds competence.

distractors.trap: Unconscious incompetence is not yet realizing the gap exists, but here the learner is now aware.
Conscious competence is performing well with deliberate effort, beyond the just-became-aware stage.
Unconscious competence is effortless mastery, the final stage, not early awareness.

explanation: The four stages run unconscious incompetence, conscious incompetence, conscious competence, unconscious competence. Realizing the gap is conscious incompetence.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes the Kotter 8-step model from ADKAR?

correct.text: Kotter operates at the organizational level through leadership action, while ADKAR diagnoses and supports change within each individual
correct.why: Kotter drives change organization-wide through leadership steps; ADKAR works at the level of each individual moving through Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement.

distractors.text: Kotter supports change within each individual, while ADKAR works at the organizational level
distractors.trap: Reverses the two, ADKAR is the individual-level model.

distractors.text: Both are three-stage models of unfreeze, change, refreeze
distractors.trap: That is the Lewin model, not Kotter or ADKAR.

distractors.text: Both are coaching frameworks like GROW and OSCAR
distractors.trap: Both are change models, not coaching frameworks.

explanation: Kotter is organization-level leadership action; ADKAR is individual-level change. Lewin is the three-stage unfreeze-change-refreeze model.

prompts: A change leader first works to unfreeze the current way of working, then moves people to the new approach, then refreezes the new state as the norm. Which model is this?

correct.why: The Lewin model is the three-stage approach of unfreezing the status quo, changing, and refreezing the new state.

distractors.trap: Kotter expands change into eight prescriptive leadership-driven steps, not three broad phases.
ADKAR is a five-element individual change model, not the unfreeze-change-refreeze sequence.
Tuckman describes team development, not organizational change phases.

explanation: Unfreeze, change, refreeze is the signature of Lewin three-stage model.

prompts: A complex, interdependent software project is already late, and the sponsor proposes adding several new developers to catch up. Which principle warns that this is likely to make the project later?

correct.why: Brooks law states that adding manpower to a late software project makes it later, because of ramp-up time and added communication overhead on interdependent work.

distractors.trap: Crashing adds resources to shorten a schedule and can work on partitionable work, but the scenario warns of the opposite effect on late interdependent work.
Parkinson law says work expands to fill the time available, not that adding people delays a late project.
The Pareto principle is the 80-20 rule about vital few causes, unrelated to staffing a late project.

explanation: Adding people to a late, complex project adds communication and onboarding overhead, the classic Brooks law trap. Crashing is the tempting distractor.

prompts: Within emotional intelligence, which statement best distinguishes self-awareness from social awareness?

correct.text: Self-awareness is insight into one own emotions and their effect on others, while social awareness is reading the emotions of others
correct.why: Self-awareness is recognizing one own internal state and its impact; social awareness is perceiving and understanding others emotions.

distractors.text: Self-awareness is reading others emotions, while social awareness is insight into one own emotions
distractors.trap: Reverses the two, reading others is social awareness.

distractors.text: Both refer to managing other people emotions directly
distractors.trap: Managing others emotions is relationship management, not these two awareness competencies.

distractors.text: Both are technical skills about tools and methods
distractors.trap: Emotional intelligence concerns emotions and relationships, not technical methods.

explanation: Self-awareness looks inward at your own state; social awareness looks outward at others. Both feed emotional intelligence.

prompts: A sponsor and a stakeholder are not the same thing. Which statement captures how they relate?

correct.text: A sponsor is one specific stakeholder who supplies resources and authority, while stakeholder is the broad class of anyone who can affect or be affected by the project
correct.why: Every sponsor is a stakeholder, but the sponsor is the narrow funding-and-backing role inside the much wider stakeholder set.

distractors.text: A stakeholder funds the project, while a sponsor merely uses the result
distractors.trap: This swaps the roles - the sponsor funds and backs the work; using the result describes a customer or end user.

distractors.text: Sponsor and stakeholder are interchangeable labels for the same group
distractors.trap: Treats a subset as the whole - sponsor is a single role, not a synonym for every interested party.

distractors.text: A sponsor performs the day-to-day project work, while a stakeholder only observes
distractors.trap: Doing the work describes the project team; sponsorship is about authority, resources, and strategic backing, not execution.

explanation: Sponsor is a specific high-authority stakeholder who provides resources and enables success; stakeholder is the umbrella category for anyone with a stake.

prompts: Which artifact identifies and classifies the people on a project, as opposed to describing how the team will involve them?

correct.why: The register records identification, assessment, and classification information about each stakeholder.

distractors.trap: This describes the strategies and approaches for involving stakeholders, not the identification list itself.
This governs how, when, and by whom information is distributed, not who the stakeholders are.
An issue log tracks current problems needing resolution, not stakeholder identity or classification.

explanation: The stakeholder register identifies and classifies stakeholders; the stakeholder engagement plan then describes how to engage them.

prompts: Two execution processes are easy to confuse. One focuses on relationships, influence, and involvement; the other on creating, distributing, and storing information. Which is the relationship-focused one?

correct.why: It centres on communicating and working with stakeholders to meet needs, address issues, and foster involvement - the relationship work.

distractors.trap: This is the information-handling counterpart: creating, distributing, storing, and retrieving project information.
Monitoring evaluates whether engagement is effective; it does not perform the active relationship work.
Planning defines the approach; the active involvement happens in Manage Stakeholder Engagement.

explanation: Manage Stakeholder Engagement works the relationships; Manage Communications moves the information. They run in parallel but answer different needs.

prompts: A respected senior engineer holds no formal title over the project team yet repeatedly sways their technical decisions through credibility and relationships. This capacity to affect decisions without a granted role is best termed what, in contrast to authority?

correct.why: Influence works through persuasion, credibility, and relationships rather than a formally granted position.

distractors.trap: Authority is the formally granted decision right tied to a role - exactly what the engineer lacks here.
Accountability is ownership of an outcome, not the ability to sway others' decisions.
Responsibility is the obligation to perform work, not informal persuasive power.

explanation: Influence is the relational, persuasive ability to affect others; authority is the positional, granted right. The engineer has influence without authority.

prompts: You publish detailed design specifications to a wiki so that hundreds of engineers can retrieve them whenever they need to. Which communication method is this?

correct.why: Pull makes large volumes of information available for recipients to access at their own discretion.

distractors.trap: Push sends information directly to specific recipients; a wiki that people choose to visit is pull, not push.
Interactive requires real-time, multidirectional exchange; reading a wiki is one-way and on demand.
This is not one of the three PMI communication methods (interactive, push, pull).

explanation: Pull = recipient retrieves at their own discretion (wiki, portal, knowledge base). Push = sent directly. Interactive = real-time exchange.

prompts: A high-stakes change must be negotiated and a shared decision reached today, so you convene a video call where parties question each other in real time. Which communication method does this scenario call for?

correct.why: Interactive is multidirectional and real-time, enabling immediate clarification and reaching shared understanding - ideal for negotiation.

distractors.trap: Push delivers a message to recipients but provides no real-time back-and-forth to negotiate the decision.
Pull lets people fetch information on demand; it cannot drive a live negotiated decision.
This is a communication form, not one of the three methods, and it lacks the immediacy the scenario needs.

explanation: Negotiation and complex problem solving need interactive (real-time, multidirectional) communication, not push or pull.

prompts: Each Friday you email a formatted status report directly to twelve named stakeholders whether or not they ask for it. Which communication method is this?

correct.why: Push sends information directly to specific recipients who need it, with no requirement that they request it.

distractors.trap: Pull would mean posting the report somewhere and letting people fetch it; here it is sent directly to named people.
A one-way emailed report has no real-time exchange, so it is not interactive.
Not one of PMI's three methods; the precise term for directed delivery is push.

explanation: Push = pushed out to specific recipients (emailed reports, memos). Pull = made available to fetch. Interactive = real-time exchange.

prompts: A project has 6 stakeholders who all communicate with one another. Two new stakeholders join, bringing the total to 8. By how many does the number of potential communication channels increase?

correct.why: Channels follow n times (n minus 1) divided by 2. At n equals 6 there are 15; at n equals 8 there are 28; the increase is 28 minus 15, which is 13.

distractors.trap: Counts only the two new people, ignoring that each new person opens a channel to every existing member.
This is the total channels at 8 stakeholders, not the increase from 6.
This is the channel count at 6 stakeholders, not the change after adding two.

explanation: Use n(n-1)/2: 6 gives 15, 8 gives 28. The growth is 13, illustrating how channels balloon as the team grows.

prompts: On a power and interest grid, a powerful regulator pays little attention to your project day to day but can halt it if displeased. Which engagement strategy fits this high-power, low-interest quadrant?

correct.why: High power with low interest calls for keeping the party satisfied without overloading them with detail they do not want.

distractors.trap: Manage closely is for high power AND high interest; this regulator has low interest, so close management would over-engage them.
Keep informed suits high interest but low power; the regulator is the reverse - high power, low interest.
Monitor is for low power and low interest; this party holds high power and must not be merely watched.

explanation: Power-interest grid: high power + low interest = keep satisfied; high power + high interest = manage closely; low power + high interest = keep informed; low power + low interest = monitor.

prompts: An end-user community is intensely interested in your rollout and asks constant questions, yet holds little formal authority over decisions. Which power and interest grid strategy applies?

correct.why: Low power with high interest calls for keeping them well informed so their enthusiasm stays constructive.

distractors.trap: Keep satisfied is for high power, low interest; this group is the opposite - low power, high interest.
Manage closely is reserved for high power and high interest; this group lacks the power half.
Monitor suits low interest; this group is highly interested and would feel ignored if merely monitored.

explanation: Low power + high interest = keep informed. They cannot move the project alone but their engagement matters, so feed them information.

prompts: A senior executive both controls your funding and follows progress in detail every week. Which power and interest grid strategy matches this high-power, high-interest stakeholder?

correct.why: High power and high interest demand the most active, fully engaged management approach.

distractors.trap: Keep satisfied is for high power but low interest; this executive is also highly interested, so passive satisfaction is not enough.
Keep informed fits low power, high interest; this executive holds high power and needs closer management.
Monitor is the minimal-effort quadrant for low power, low interest - wholly wrong for a powerful, engaged sponsor.

explanation: High power + high interest = manage closely. This quadrant gets your fullest engagement effort.

prompts: Using the salience model, a group has urgent, time-critical demands and a legitimate claim on the project, but no power to enforce anything. Which salience class are they?

correct.why: Dependent stakeholders have legitimacy and urgency but lack power, so they depend on others to act on their behalf.

distractors.trap: Dangerous combines power and urgency without legitimacy; this group has legitimacy but no power.
Dominant combines power and legitimacy; this group is missing power, which is the defining gap.
Definitive holds all three attributes; this group lacks power, so it cannot be definitive.

explanation: Salience two-attribute classes: dominant = power + legitimacy; dangerous = power + urgency; dependent = legitimacy + urgency (no power).

prompts: A faction has the power to disrupt your project and presses urgent demands, but it has no legitimate stake in the outcome. In the salience model this faction is which class?

correct.why: Power plus urgency without legitimacy defines the dangerous class - capable of coercive action with no rightful claim.

distractors.trap: Dependent has legitimacy and urgency but no power; this faction has power and lacks legitimacy - the reverse profile.
Dominant pairs power with legitimacy; the faction lacks legitimacy, which is the whole point.
Demanding is a latent class with urgency only; this faction also wields power, so it is more than demanding.

explanation: Dangerous = power + urgency, no legitimacy. The missing legitimacy is what makes the urgent, powerful pressure dangerous rather than dominant.

prompts: A dangerous stakeholder is defined by exactly two of the three salience attributes. Select the two attributes a dangerous stakeholder possesses.

correct.why: A dangerous stakeholder can compel action, so power is present.
A dangerous stakeholder presses time-critical demands, so urgency is present.

distractors.trap: Legitimacy is precisely the attribute a dangerous stakeholder lacks; adding it would make them definitive.

explanation: Dangerous = power + urgency, with legitimacy absent. The missing legitimacy is what distinguishes it from the definitive class.

prompts: A stakeholder actively works to block your project and lobbies others against it. On the engagement-level scale, which level describes this current state?

correct.why: Resistant stakeholders are aware of the project and opposed to the change or its outcomes.

distractors.trap: Unaware means the person does not yet know about the project; this stakeholder clearly knows and opposes it.
Neutral is aware but neither supportive nor opposed; active blocking is opposition, not neutrality.
Leading stakeholders actively champion success - the opposite of someone trying to derail it.

explanation: Engagement levels run unaware, resistant, neutral, supportive, leading. Active opposition is resistant.

prompts: Four of these belong to the standard stakeholder engagement-level scale. Which one does NOT?

correct.why: Indifferent is not a defined engagement level; the neutral level already covers the aware-but-uncommitted state.

distractors.trap: Unaware is the first defined engagement level - the stakeholder does not yet know about the project.
Supportive is a defined level - aware and in favour of the project and its outcomes.
Leading is the top defined level - actively engaged in ensuring success.

explanation: The five engagement levels are unaware, resistant, neutral, supportive, and leading. Indifferent is not one of them.

prompts: Three of these are attributes in the salience model. Which item is NOT a salience attribute?

correct.why: Interest is an axis of the power-interest grid, not one of the salience model's three attributes.

distractors.trap: Power is one of the three salience attributes - the ability to impose will on the project.
Legitimacy is a salience attribute - the validity of the stakeholder claim.
Urgency is a salience attribute - the time-critical nature of the claim.

explanation: Salience attributes are power, legitimacy, and urgency. Interest belongs to the power-interest grid, a different model.

prompts: Three of the following are recognised power-interest grid strategies. Which option is NOT one of them?

correct.why: This is not a standard power-interest quadrant label; the four are manage closely, keep satisfied, keep informed, and monitor.

distractors.trap: Keep satisfied is the genuine strategy for the high-power, low-interest quadrant.
Keep informed is the genuine strategy for the low-power, high-interest quadrant.
Monitor is the genuine strategy for the low-power, low-interest quadrant.

explanation: The four grid strategies are manage closely, keep satisfied, keep informed, and monitor. Manage at a distance is invented.

prompts: Stakeholder influence in a project can run in several directions. Three of these are recognised directions of influence. Which one is NOT?

correct.why: Inward is not a standard direction of influence; the recognised directions are upward, downward, outward, and sideward.

distractors.trap: Upward is genuine - influencing senior management and sponsors.
Downward is genuine - influencing the team and those one directs.
Sideward is genuine - influencing peers and fellow project managers.

explanation: Directions of influence are upward, downward, outward, and sideward. Inward is not part of the set.

prompts: One process defines the approach for involving stakeholders; another carries that approach out during execution. Which is the one that defines the approach?

correct.why: Planning develops the approaches for involving stakeholders based on their needs and impact, before the work is executed.

distractors.trap: This executes the approach during the work; it does not define it.
This evaluates and adjusts engagement effectiveness; it is a controlling activity, not the planning of the approach.
Identification finds and registers stakeholders but does not develop the engagement approach itself.

explanation: Plan Stakeholder Engagement sets the approach; Manage carries it out; Monitor checks and tailors it.

prompts: A project manager senses unspoken rivalries between two departments and quietly maps who really sways decisions before proposing a change. This skill of reading informal power dynamics is best termed what - as distinct from the power itself?

correct.why: Political awareness is the ability to recognise and interpret informal power, interests, and relationships that shape decisions.

distractors.trap: Power is the actual capacity to influence outcomes; the scenario describes perceiving power, not holding it.
Authority is formally granted decision rights, not the perceptive skill of reading the room.
Organizational politics is the underlying dynamic being read; political awareness is the skill of reading it.

explanation: Political awareness = the skill of understanding influence patterns. Power = the actual capacity to influence. The PM is exercising awareness, not power.

prompts: A team reaches a decision quickly because nobody wants to disturb the friendly mood, so genuine doubts go unspoken and a weak option passes. This pattern is best identified as what, not as consensus?

correct.why: Groupthink is when the desire for harmony or conformity suppresses critical thinking and yields poor decisions.

distractors.trap: Consensus is a healthy outcome where members can support a decision after real deliberation - not suppressed dissent.
Unanimity means everyone genuinely agrees; here agreement is only apparent because doubts were withheld.
Collaboration is constructive joint work; the scenario describes dysfunctional conformity, the opposite of healthy collaboration.

explanation: Groupthink = conformity that silences critical thinking. Consensus is healthy support for a decision; unanimity is full agreement. The stem describes groupthink.

prompts: A facilitator announces that the goal is a decision everyone can live with and actively support, even if it is not each person's first choice. Which decision outcome is being sought?

correct.why: Consensus means participants can support the chosen option even when it is not their personal favourite.

distractors.trap: Unanimity requires everyone to fully agree; the facilitator deliberately asked only for support, not full agreement.
Majority lets the larger faction win regardless of whether the rest can support it; that is not what is described.
Plurality picks the most-voted option even without a majority; the stem seeks broad supportability, not a vote tally.

explanation: Consensus = all can support the decision. Unanimity = all fully agree. The facilitator is seeking consensus.

prompts: Two parties hold competing interests and must reach an agreement they can both accept through give and take. Which activity is this, as distinct from neutrally guiding a group through a process?

correct.why: Negotiation resolves disputes between parties with competing interests by bargaining toward an agreement.

distractors.trap: Facilitation guides group interaction without bargaining over positions; the scenario centres on competing interests being traded.
Brainstorming generates ideas, it does not resolve a dispute between opposing parties.
The scenario has the two parties bargaining directly, with no neutral third party deciding or guiding between them.

explanation: Negotiation = reaching agreement between parties with competing interests. Facilitation = guiding group process neutrally. The stem describes negotiation.

prompts: On a RACI chart, which pairing describes parties who give input before action and parties who are simply kept updated after the fact - rather than those who own the work or its outcome?

correct.why: Consulted parties provide input beforehand; informed parties receive updates afterward - both are participation roles, not ownership roles.

distractors.trap: Responsible does the work and accountable owns the result; these are ownership roles, not the input-and-update roles asked for.
Mixes an ownership role (accountable) with a participation role; the question asks for the two participation roles only.
Pairs an ownership role (responsible) with a participation role; the two participation roles are consulted and informed.

explanation: RACI: Responsible and Accountable own the work and result; Consulted and Informed describe communication participation - input before and updates after.

prompts: Every key stakeholder is a stakeholder, but the reverse is not true. What makes a stakeholder a key stakeholder?

correct.text: The ability to materially affect change, sway decisions, or be significantly impacted by outcomes
correct.why: Key stakeholders are the subset with high influence, decision power, or significant impact on or from the project.

distractors.text: Being listed anywhere in the stakeholder register
distractors.trap: Mere registration makes someone a stakeholder, not necessarily a key one; key status is about influence and impact.

distractors.text: Holding a formal contract with the performing organisation
distractors.trap: A contract does not by itself confer the influence or impact that defines a key stakeholder.
Attendance is incidental; key status depends on influence and impact, not meeting presence.

explanation: Key stakeholder is the high-influence, high-impact subset of stakeholders; every stakeholder is in the register, but only some are key.

prompts: A project manager continually works to align what stakeholders believe the project will deliver with what it realistically can deliver and when. This focused practice is best named what?

correct.why: Expectation management is the ongoing work of aligning stakeholder expectations with realistic deliverables and timing.

distractors.trap: Engagement is the broader practice of involving stakeholders; the stem describes the narrower task of aligning expectations.
Scope management controls what work is included; it is not specifically about aligning beliefs with reality.
Change control assesses and approves changes to baselines, not the alignment of perceptions with deliverable reality.

explanation: Expectation management specifically aligns what stakeholders expect with what can realistically be delivered; engagement is the wider relationship effort.

prompts: Distributed teams brainstorm ideas individually and then share them electronically rather than shouting them out in a live room. Which technique is this, as opposed to classic brainstorming?

correct.why: Brain-netting is the electronic, often asynchronous variation where participants generate ideas individually and share them online.

distractors.trap: Classic brainstorming is a live, facilitated group session - exactly what the stem contrasts against.
A focus group is a facilitated live discussion to gather perceptions, not asynchronous electronic idea sharing.
Nominal group technique ranks and votes on ideas in a structured session; it is not defined by electronic remote sharing.

explanation: Brain-netting = electronic, individual-then-shared idea generation. Brainstorming = live group session. The remote electronic variation is brain-netting.

prompts: A persona and a stakeholder are easy to mix up. Which statement correctly separates them?

correct.text: A persona is a representative archetype of a user group; a stakeholder is an actual person, group, or organisation affected by or able to influence the work
correct.why: Personas are fictional composites for design empathy; stakeholders are real parties with real stakes.

distractors.text: A persona is a real individual; a stakeholder is a fictional archetype
distractors.trap: Reverses the two - the persona is the archetype, the stakeholder is the real party.

distractors.text: They are identical concepts used in different documents
distractors.trap: They are distinct: one is a modelled user profile, the other an actual interested party.

distractors.text: A persona always holds decision authority; a stakeholder never does
distractors.trap: Fabricates an authority rule; some stakeholders hold authority, and personas hold none because they are not real.

explanation: Persona = a realistic archetype representing a user or stakeholder group. Stakeholder = an actual person or organisation with a stake.

prompts: Using the salience model, a recently retired board member still holds considerable power but currently presses no urgent demand and has no active legitimate claim on the project. Which class are they?

correct.why: Dormant stakeholders hold power alone, without active legitimacy or urgency, so their power lies unused for now.

distractors.trap: Dominant needs power plus legitimacy; this person has power but no active legitimate claim.
Dangerous needs power plus urgency; there is no urgent demand here.
Definitive requires all three attributes; only power is present.

explanation: Latent classes hold one attribute: dormant = power only, discretionary = legitimacy only, demanding = urgency only. Power alone is dormant.

prompts: A stakeholder is classified as dominant in the salience model. Select the two attributes a dominant stakeholder possesses.

correct.why: Dominant stakeholders can impose their will, so power is present.
Dominant stakeholders have a valid, recognised claim, so legitimacy is present.

distractors.trap: Urgency is the attribute a dominant stakeholder lacks; adding it would make them definitive.
Attitude is a stakeholder-cube dimension, not a salience attribute at all.

explanation: Dominant = power + legitimacy, no urgency. Add urgency and the stakeholder becomes definitive (all three).

prompts: A dashboard and a status report present project information differently. Which statement captures the contrast?

correct.text: A dashboard gives at-a-glance visual indicators of current status and trends; a status report provides broader narrative explanation and context
correct.why: Dashboards emphasise quick visual signals; status reports add the written narrative behind them.

distractors.text: A dashboard is a written narrative; a status report is a set of visual gauges
distractors.trap: Reverses the two - the dashboard is the visual at-a-glance view, the report carries the narrative.

distractors.text: A dashboard is only used at closure; a status report only during planning
distractors.trap: Fabricates a timing rule; both are used throughout execution and monitoring.

distractors.text: They are identical and the words can be swapped freely
distractors.trap: They differ in form and depth: visual snapshot versus narrative report.

explanation: Dashboard = visual, at-a-glance status and trends. Status report = fuller narrative and context. Both communicate performance, in different forms.

prompts: A stakeholder has a clearly legitimate interest in the project but no power to act on it and feels no time pressure about it. In the salience model, which class are they?

correct.why: Discretionary stakeholders hold legitimacy alone, without power or urgency, so attention to them is largely at the team's discretion.

distractors.trap: Dependent needs legitimacy plus urgency; here there is no urgency, only legitimacy.
Dominant needs power plus legitimacy; this stakeholder has no power.
Demanding is urgency alone; this stakeholder has legitimacy, not urgency.

explanation: Discretionary = legitimacy only. With neither power nor urgency, engaging them is a discretionary choice rather than a necessity.
